<!-- Topic 3: Lab — Array-Backed Stack -->
<!-- Total slides: 10 -->

# Lab — Array-Backed Stack

---

## Can we build a stack from an array? {.smaller}

- A stack is a LIFO collection: the last item pushed is the first item popped
- An array provides the storage; functions provide the interface

::: notes
Total slides: 10
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## What is a stack?

Five operations define a stack:

- `push` — add a value to the top
- `pop` — remove and return the top value
- `peek` — read the top value without removing it
- `isFull` — is there room for another push?
- `isEmpty` — is there anything to pop?

::: notes
[Graphic suggestion: vertical stack diagram with labeled arrows — push pointing down onto the top, pop pointing up off the top, peek pointing right at the top element without disturbing it]
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## The data

```{.cpp}
const int MAX = 10;

int stack[MAX];
int top = -1;       // -1 means the stack is empty
```

- `top` tracks the index of the current top element
- When `top == -1` the stack is empty; when `top == MAX - 1` it is full
- Every function receives `stack` and `top` as parameters — there are no globals

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Function signatures

```{.cpp}
bool isFull(int top);
bool isEmpty(int top);
void push(int stack[], int& top, int value);
int  pop(int stack[], int& top);
int  peek(int stack[], int top);
```

- `top` is passed by reference to `push` and `pop` — they change it
- `top` is passed by value to `peek`, `isFull`, and `isEmpty` — read-only

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Task — implement push and isFull

```{.cpp}
bool isFull(int top) {
    // your code here
}

void push(int stack[], int& top, int value) {
    // your code here
}
```

Work with your partner. Five minutes.

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## Solution — push and isFull

```{.cpp}
bool isFull(int top) {
    return top == MAX - 1;
}

void push(int stack[], int& top, int value) {
    if (isFull(top)) return;
    stack[++top] = value;   // increment top, then store
}
```

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Task — pop, peek, and isEmpty

```{.cpp}
bool isEmpty(int top) {
    // your code here
}

int pop(int stack[], int& top) {
    // your code here
}

int peek(int stack[], int top) {
    // your code here
}
```

Five minutes.

<!-- Slide 7 -->

---

## Solution — pop, peek, and isEmpty

```{.cpp}
bool isEmpty(int top) {
    return top == -1;
}

int pop(int stack[], int& top) {
    if (isEmpty(top)) return -1;
    return stack[top--];    // return value, then decrement
}

int peek(int stack[], int top) {
    return stack[top];
}
```

<!-- Slide 8 -->

---

## Putting it together

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

const int MAX = 10;

bool isFull(int top);
bool isEmpty(int top);
void push(int stack[], int& top, int value);
int  pop(int stack[], int& top);
int  peek(int stack[], int top);

int main() {
    int stack[MAX];
    int top = -1;

    push(stack, top, 10);
    push(stack, top, 20);
    push(stack, top, 30);

    cout << peek(stack, top) << "\n";   // 30
    cout << pop(stack, top)  << "\n";   // 30
    cout << pop(stack, top)  << "\n";   // 20
    return 0;
}

// ... function definitions from slides above
```

<!-- Slide 9 -->

---

## Summary

- A stack uses an array for storage and a `top` index to track the current top element
- The five-function API (push, pop, peek, isFull, isEmpty) is a first example of separating behavior from data

<!-- Slide 10 -->
